cases.name: implemented-design-polish

cases.task: Review this UI plan before implementation and score the design dimensions with recommendations.

cases.name: design-system

cases.name: design-variants
cases.task: Show me several visual directions for this feature and let me compare options before choosing.

cases.name: product-brainstorm

cases.name: spec-plus-browser-validation

cases.task: Rethink this plan from a founder perspective and tell me whether the scope is ambitious enough.

cases.name: engineering-plan-review

cases.name: auto-plan-review

cases.name: pre-landing-review
cases.task: Review this PR before merge for SQL safety, trust boundaries, and production bugs.

cases.name: root-cause-debug
cases.task: Debug why this job started failing and do not patch anything until you find the root cause.

cases.name: security-audit
cases.task: Run an infrastructure-first security audit with secrets, dependency supply chain, OWASP, and STRIDE coverage.

cases.name: ship-pr
cases.task: Sync main, run tests, update changelog, push this branch, and create a PR.

cases.name: land-and-deploy
cases.task: Merge the approved PR, wait for CI and deploy, then verify production health.

cases.task: Detect our deploy platform and write the production URL, health checks, and deploy commands into project docs.

cases.name: post-deploy-monitor
cases.task: Watch the live app after deploy for console errors, page failures, and regressions.

cases.name: performance-regression
cases.task: Establish a page-load and Core Web Vitals baseline and compare this PR against it.

cases.name: docs-after-release
cases.task: Update README, architecture docs, and changelog to match what just shipped.

cases.name: weekly-retro

tools.purpose: Run the spec-qa gstack workflow: Founder/operator workflow for product shaping and browser validation. Turns a rough feature request into a concrete spec, pressure-tests the spec, and validates the critical path in a real browser when a URL is available. Use when asked to "shape this feature", "turn this into a spec", "review the plan then test it", "spec and QA this", or "go from idea to browser verification". Proactively suggest when the user is bouncing between product-definition work and browser QA and wants one artifact-driven workflow.

arguments.request: Open http://localhost:3000, click through the signup flow, and capture screenshots of any bug evidence.

choice.rationale: The user wants to navigate to a URL, interact with elements (click through signup flow), and take screenshots of bugs. This is a perfect match for the browse skill which is designed for 'Navigate any URL, interact with elements, verify page state, diff before/after actions, take annotated screenshots'. The browse skill explicitly mentions use cases like 'test a feature', 'dogfood a user flow', and 'file a bug with evidence'.

arguments.request: QA this staging site, fix the bugs you find, add regression tests, and commit each fix atomically.

choice.rationale: The user is asking to QA test a site, fix bugs found, and commit fixes atomically. The gstack_qa skill is specifically designed for this workflow: 'Systematically QA test a web application and fix bugs found. Runs QA testing, then iteratively fixes bugs in source code, committing each fix atomically and re-verifying.' This matches the request perfectly, including the atomic commit requirement and the test-fix-verify loop.

arguments.request: Test this app and give me a bug report with repro steps, but do not edit any files.

choice.rationale: The user explicitly wants QA testing with a bug report and reproduction steps, but specifically requests NO file edits. The gstack_qa_only skill is designed exactly for this use case - it performs systematic QA testing and produces a structured report with health score, screenshots, and repro steps, but never fixes anything. The regular /qa skill would test AND fix bugs, which contradicts the user's requirement.

arguments.request: Audit the implemented dashboard UI for spacing, hierarchy, and visual slop, then fix the issues.

choice.rationale: The user wants to audit and fix visual issues (spacing, hierarchy, visual slop) in an already-implemented dashboard UI. The gstack_design_review skill is specifically designed for 'Designer's eye QA: finds visual inconsistency, spacing issues, hierarchy problems, AI slop patterns' and 'iteratively fixes issues in source code, committing each fix atomically and re-verifying with before/after screenshots.' This matches the request perfectly as it's about auditing and fixing a live site's visual quality.

arguments.request: Review this UI plan before implementation and score the design dimensions with recommendations.

choice.rationale: The user is asking to review a UI plan before implementation. The plan-design-review skill is specifically designed for this: 'Designer's eye plan review — interactive, like CEO and Eng review. Rates each design dimension 0-10, explains what would make it a 10, then fixes the plan to get there. Works in plan mode.' This matches the request to review and score design dimensions with recommendations before implementation.

arguments.request: Create a design system and DESIGN.md for a new B2B workflow product.

choice.rationale: The user explicitly requests creating a design system and DESIGN.md for a new product. The gstack_design_consultation skill is specifically designed for this purpose: it 'understands your product, researches the landscape, proposes a complete design system (aesthetic, typography, color, layout, spacing, motion), and generates font+color preview pages. Creates DESIGN.md as your project's design source of truth.' The use_when criteria explicitly mentions 'create DESIGN.md' and 'starting a new project's UI with no existing design system or DESIGN.md', which perfectly matches this request.

arguments.request: Show me several visual directions for this feature and let me compare options before choosing.

choice.rationale: The user wants to explore multiple design variants and compare them before making a decision. This matches the design-shotgun workflow perfectly, which generates multiple AI design variants, opens a comparison board, collects structured feedback, and iterates. The request contains key phrases like 'several visual directions', 'compare options', and 'before choosing' which align with the design exploration and variant comparison purpose of this skill.

arguments.request: I have an idea for a restaurant waitlist tool. Help me pressure-test whether it is worth building.

choice.rationale: The user is exploring a new product idea and wants to pressure-test whether it's worth building. This matches the office-hours workflow perfectly, which is designed for brainstorming and validating product ideas through forcing questions (startup mode) or design thinking (builder mode). The workflow will expose demand reality, status quo, desperate specificity, narrowest wedge, observation, and future-fit before any code is written. This is the appropriate pre-planning stage tool.

arguments.request: Turn this rough feature request into a spec, pressure-test it, then validate the critical path in the browser.

choice.rationale: The user's request explicitly matches the spec-qa workflow's purpose: 'Turns a rough feature request into a concrete spec, pressure-tests the spec, and validates the critical path in a real browser'. The use_when criteria includes 'turn this into a spec', 'review the plan then test it', and 'spec and QA this', which directly align with the user's request to create a spec, pressure-test it, and validate in browser.

arguments.request: Rethink this plan from a founder perspective and tell me whether the scope is ambitious enough.

choice.rationale: The user is explicitly asking for a founder/CEO perspective on whether a plan's scope is ambitious enough. This directly matches the gstack_plan_ceo_review workflow, which is designed for 'CEO/founder-mode plan review' that 'rethink[s] the problem, find[s] the 10-star product, challenge[s] premises, expand[s] scope when it creates a better product.'
